title: UCB — Immunology — Synthesis
html:
Bimzelx Cross-Study Synthesis -- ZoomRx HCP-Patient Conversation Research

Data Sources

This synthesis draws on the following ZoomRx deliverables for UCB Bimzelx:

  1. HCP-Patient Conversations Interim Report (June 2025) -- 19 conversations, 19 HCPs, May 20 - June 9, 2025
  2. HCP-Patient Conversations Interim Report Ver2 (June 17, 2025) -- Updated analysis with additional conversations
  3. HCP-Patient Conversations Final Report (July 3, 2025) -- 30 conversations, 26 HCPs, May 20 - June 19, 2025
  4. HCP-Patient Conversations Final Report v6 (July 23, 2025) -- Refined version for internal teams
  5. HCP-Patient Conversations Final Report (September 4, 2025) -- Updated version with behavioral science insights and segment analysis
  6. HCP-Patient Conversations Proposal (January 2025) -- Original study design and methodology

All findings are directional given the qualitative nature of the research and modest sample sizes. The study focused on Dermatologists and NP/PAs from UCB's target list treating adult patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (PsO) and/or active psoriatic arthritis (PsA).

Pattern 1: The Efficacy-Safety Paradox -- What Drives Rx Is Not What Gets Discussed

Consistent finding across all waves: Efficacy is the #1 cited reason for prescribing across all products, yet HCPs spend disproportionately little time discussing efficacy with patients compared to safety and dosing.

Quantitative pattern:

  • Efficacy mentioned in ~80% of all conversations but occupied only ~10% of conversation time
  • Safety/tolerability occupied ~20-30% of conversation time in Bimzelx-specific discussions
  • Dosing/administration discussed in most conversations with moderate time allocation
  • When asked post-conversation, HCPs universally cited efficacy as their primary Rx reason

What this means: HCPs make prescribing decisions based on efficacy data they have internalized from clinical literature, peer discussions, and rep interactions -- not from what they discuss with the patient in the room. The patient-facing conversation is primarily about de-risking the decision (safety reassurance, dosing logistics) and gaining practical buy-in. This has critical implications for UCB commercial strategy: improving HCP efficacy conviction happens outside the patient conversation (via medical affairs, KOL engagement, congress presentations, rep detailing); improving patient acceptance happens during the conversation (via safety messaging, dosing simplification, and access support).

Evolution across waves: This pattern was present from the earliest interim report (19 conversations) and remained stable through the final report (30 conversations), suggesting it is structural rather than sample-dependent.

Pattern 2: The Three-Layer Decision Funnel -- Route, Product, Details

Consistent finding across all waves: Treatment decisions follow a predictable three-layer funnel, with different decision-makers at each layer.

Layer 1: Treatment history and symptom burden (jointly assessed)

  • HCPs begin every conversation reviewing prior treatments (topicals, orals, biologics), treatment response, adherence, and tolerance
  • Current symptoms, QoL impact, and urgency are assessed through BSA, NRS, and patient-reported outcomes
  • This layer sets the clinical stage but rarely determines the specific product

Layer 2: Route of administration -- largely PATIENT-driven

  • In 13 of 30 conversations, patients directly expressed preference between oral and injectable
  • In 17 of 30, patient indirect cues (high symptom burden, urgency, desire for stronger treatment) prompted HCPs to skip oral discussion and suggest injectables directly
  • Needle aversion is the single most powerful patient-side factor, capable of overriding HCP clinical judgment
  • HCPs almost never push back against a patient who chooses oral over injectable, even when the HCP believes injectables would be more effective

Layer 3: Product selection -- largely HCP-driven

  • Once route is decided, 23 of 30 HCPs presented a SINGLE product to the patient
  • Only 7 conversations featured multiple products within the same route
  • HCPs choose the product based on personal clinical experience, familiarity, and comfort
  • "I've used this a lot with my patients" is a common HCP framing that caters to patient need for social proof

Implication: The commercial battleground is not patient awareness or preference -- it is HCP default product selection. Bimzelx must become the product that HCPs reach for first when they present a single injectable option. This requires sustained engagement at the HCP level, not patient-directed advertising alone.

Pattern 3: HCP Cognitive Biases Systematically Disadvantage Newer Products

Identified in the behavioral science analysis (September 2025 report): Three reinforcing cognitive biases shape HCP behavior in ways that structurally disadvantage Bimzelx as the newer entrant:

Effort Aversion: HCPs have limited time with patients and want to gain buy-in quickly while minimizing discordance. Introducing a new product with additional safety disclosures (mood screening, candidiasis, GI screening) requires more effort than defaulting to a familiar product. The suicidal ideation disclosure is a prime example: most HCPs are not clinically concerned about the risk, but they avoid Bimzelx partly because explaining it takes time and creates patient anxiety that must be managed.

Familiarity Bias: HCPs default to products they have prescribed before because they can speak from personal experience, which builds patient trust more effectively than quoting trial data. "I've put four people on it, and they've all been people like you... and they're pretty clear" is the kind of personal endorsement that only comes with prescribing experience. This creates a chicken-and-egg problem for Bimzelx: HCPs need experience to develop comfort, but they need comfort to gain experience.

Framing Effect: The order in which treatment options are presented shapes patient receptivity. When an HCP presents a weaker oral option first, then a stronger injectable, patients may anchor on the "middle option." When safety information is front-loaded before efficacy (as often happens with Bimzelx due to label requirements), patients may anchor on risk rather than benefit.

Cross-wave consistency: These biases were observable from the interim data but were formally codified in the September 2025 behavioral science analysis. The finding that even Bimzelx Believers (4 of 7) cited safety concerns for not prescribing suggests these biases operate even among theoretically favorable HCPs.

Pattern 4: The Bimzelx Safety Discussion Has a Specific Architecture

Consistent finding across Bimzelx-specific conversations (n=10 in final report): When Bimzelx is discussed, safety conversations follow a predictable pattern with distinct components that vary in their impact on prescribing.

Components of the Bimzelx safety discussion:

Safety Topic Frequency How Discussed Impact on Rx
Depression/Suicide risk 6/10 convos Framed as rare, unclear if drug-related, based on single trial event. Some HCPs softly screen for depression/self-harm history. Minimal -- did not change prescribing in any observed case
GI upset / IBD 4/10 convos HCPs actively screen with direct questions about GI history, UC, Crohn's. Positioned as IL-17 class concern. HIGH -- directly diverted 1 patient from Bimzelx to Skyrizi; active screening in others
Candidiasis 4/10 convos Consistently reassured: "very treatable," "5-7 day pill," "once treated doesn't come back." 9% incidence cited. Low -- reassurance is effective, no observed Rx change
Infection risk 4/10 convos Routine biologic class disclosure. Immunosuppression framed as manageable with pre-screening. Negligible -- standard biologic disclosure

Key insight: GI history is the only safety concern that demonstrably changes prescribing behavior. The suicide risk warning creates conversational burden (effort aversion) but does not change clinical decisions. Candidiasis is effectively de-risked by HCPs through strong reassurance. This hierarchy should inform UCB's safety messaging priorities: provide HCPs with efficient scripts for suicide risk disclosure (reduce effort burden) and clear GI screening guidance (help HCPs make informed decisions rather than defaulting away).

Segment variation: Both Physician Assistants who prescribed Bimzelx did not mention depression risk or go into detailed side effects, while Dermatologists focused more on comprehensive safety disclosure. This suggests NP/PAs may be less encumbered by the safety perception barrier, representing a potentially more receptive target for Bimzelx adoption.

Pattern 5: Bimzelx Occupies a "Later Line" Position That Is Both a Constraint and a Signal

Consistent finding across waves: Bimzelx is positioned as a later-line biologic option, used when other therapies have failed. In the treatment sequence analysis:

  • In all 5 cases where an initial injectable failed to provide adequate relief, IL-17 inhibitors were the next step (Taltz in 2, Bimzelx in 3)
  • One verbatim explicitly states: "Bimzelx is often used as a last resort. It serves as a later-line biologic option for those who have experienced treatment failure with other biologic agents"
  • A follow-up patient who had been on Cosentyx (switched due to insurance issues) reported after 8 weeks on Bimzelx: "I liked Cosentyx... but I've been on it for eight weeks now and I think I've had three injections, and it's, I am really liking it"

The constraint: Later-line positioning limits the addressable patient volume. If Bimzelx is only considered after Skyrizi, Taltz, or Cosentyx failure, the total opportunity is a fraction of the market.

The signal: Being reserved for later lines also signals that HCPs view Bimzelx as potent -- something saved for when other options fail. This "strongest available" perception is an asset that can be repositioned. The BE BOLD data (superiority over Skyrizi in PsA) provides clinical rationale for moving Bimzelx earlier in the treatment sequence.

Specific switching patterns observed:

  • Skyrizi dissatisfaction (limited relief, injection discomfort) prompting switch to Bimzelx
  • Cosentyx discontinuation (insurance issues) with Bimzelx as successor (patient reported high satisfaction)
  • Otezla/Sotyktu failure escalating to Bimzelx when IL-23 options were not suitable (e.g., patient had negative peer experience with Skyrizi)

Pattern 6: Patient Decision-Making Is Governed by Four Behavioral Principles

Codified in the September 2025 behavioral science analysis: Patient behavior during treatment discussions follows four predictable principles that cut across all conversations, not just Bimzelx-specific ones.

1. Need for Control: Patients want normalcy on their own terms. This encompasses symptom control, reduced mental burden, and reduced lifestyle disruption (from both the disease and the treatment). Patients gravitate toward whatever option promises the most control with the least change. For Bimzelx, fast onset of action and high clearance rates serve this need well; for IL-23s, infrequent dosing and established track record serve it differently.

2. Cognitive Overload: Patients struggle with too much data and too many choices. They revert to heuristics (shortcuts) like anchoring on a single metric (injection frequency or efficacy), deferring to the HCP ("what would you do?"), or defaulting to a peer's experience. This is why 23/30 HCPs present only one product -- they intuitively understand that choice overload paralyzes patients.

3. Base Rate Neglect / Social Proof: Patients weight a single anecdotal experience (especially from a known peer) more heavily than large-scale clinical data. One patient rejected Skyrizi because "you talked to one of your friends who had been on Skyrizi for something else, and they had a bad experience." No amount of PASI data could overcome this single peer report. Conversely, HCPs leverage social proof by saying "I've used this a lot with my patients" to build confidence.

4. Catastrophic Thinking: When patients learn about injectables, they immediately picture the worst-case scenario (painful daily injections). When they hear about side effects, they imagine the most severe form (equating "risk of depressive thoughts" with "I'll commit suicide"). This creates an asymmetric information problem: negative information has outsized impact compared to positive information.

Implication for Bimzelx: The catastrophic thinking pattern is particularly challenging for Bimzelx because of the suicidal ideation label warning. Even though HCPs de-risk it effectively ("one patient in the clinical trial, hard to tell if drug-related"), the mere mention triggers catastrophic thinking in patients. UCB needs HCPs to frame this disclosure within a broader safety context that pre-empts catastrophic interpretation.

Pattern 7: Manufacturer Interaction Has Limited Prescribing Conversion

Data from the screener/survey component: HCPs reported manufacturer interactions across multiple companies, with AbbVie and Eli Lilly most frequently cited, followed by Janssen, Amgen, Novartis, BMS, Pfizer, and UCB.

UCB-specific finding: 12 of 30 HCPs reported a UCB (Bimzelx) interaction in the past month. Distribution was even across segments: 4 Bimzelx Believers, 4 Dabblers, 4 Convert IL-17A Writers. Of these 12, only 4 prescribed Bimzelx -- a 33% conversion rate from recent interaction to Rx.

Messages discussed during UCB interactions: Efficacy (PASI scores, quick onset, high ACR), indication coverage (PsO, PsA), safety, coverage/prior authorization, and patient assistance programs.

What this signals: A single rep interaction is insufficient to shift prescribing behavior, particularly when competing against entrenched familiarity bias. The 8 HCPs who had UCB interactions but did not prescribe Bimzelx cited safety concerns, needle aversion, and access challenges -- the same barriers documented in the conversation analysis. This suggests that rep interactions are not effectively addressing the specific barriers that prevent conversion.

Synthesis: The Commercial Opportunity Framework

Across all waves and versions of the HCP-Pt research, five actionable opportunities emerge:

  1. Reduce effort burden for safety disclosure: Create concise, approved HCP scripts for the three safety disclosures (suicidal ideation, candidiasis, GI screening) that take <60 seconds each. Effort aversion is addressable through better tools.

  2. Build prescribing experience through targeted programs: Sampling programs, bridge programs, and guaranteed-access pathways can break the familiarity bias chicken-and-egg problem. Every first prescription creates future familiarity.

  3. Leverage the "speed + joints" narrative for patient selection: Rather than positioning Bimzelx as a broad competitor to Skyrizi, target the definable patient archetype: moderate-to-severe PsO with joint involvement and/or high urgency for clearance.

  4. Equip HCPs with social proof stories: The most powerful prescribing endorsement is "I've used this with patients like you." UCB should accelerate the accumulation and dissemination of personal success stories from prescribing HCPs, particularly among the Dabbler and Convert segments.

  5. Address access parity: Insurance/prior authorization friction was cited as a barrier multiple times. Until Bimzelx achieves access parity with Cosentyx, Taltz, and Skyrizi, even convinced HCPs will default to the easier-to-access product.

Components of the Bimzelx safety discussion:

Depression/Suicide risk

Framed as rare, unclear if drug-related, based on single trial event. Some HCPs softly screen for depression/self-harm history.

This suggests NP/PAs may be less encumbered by the safety perception barrier, representing a potentially more receptive target for Bimzelx adoption.

Pattern 5: Bimzelx Occupies a "Later Line" Position That Is Both a Constraint and a Signal
Consistent finding across waves: Bimzelx is positioned as a later-line biologic option, used when other therapies have failed. In the treatment sequence analysis:

In all 5 cases where an initial injectable failed to provide adequate relief, IL-17 inhibitors were the next step (Taltz in 2, Bimzelx in 3)
One verbatim explicitly states: "Bimzelx is often used as a last resort. It serves as a later-line biologic option for those who have experienced treatment failure with other biologic agents"
A follow-up patient who had been on Cosentyx (switched due to insurance issues) reported after 8 weeks on Bimzelx: "I liked Cosentyx... but I've been on it for eight weeks now and I think I've had three injections, and it's, I am really liking it"

The signal: Being reserved for later lines also signals that HCPs view Bimzelx as potent -- something saved for when other options fail. This "strongest available" perception is an asset that can be repositioned. The BE BOLD data (superiority over Skyrizi in PsA) provides clinical rationale for moving Bimzelx earlier in the treatment sequence.
Specific switching patterns observed:

Skyrizi dissatisfaction (limited relief, injection discomfort) prompting switch to Bimzelx
Cosentyx discontinuation (insurance issues) with Bimzelx as successor (patient reported high satisfaction)
Otezla/Sotyktu failure escalating to Bimzelx when IL-23 options were not suitable (e.g., patient had negative peer experience with Skyrizi)


Pattern 6: Patient Decision-Making Is Governed by Four Behavioral Principles
Codified in the September 2025 behavioral science analysis: Patient behavior during treatment discussions follows four predictable principles that cut across all conversations, not just Bimzelx-specific ones.
1. Need for Control: Patients want normalcy on their own terms. This encompasses symptom control, reduced mental burden, and reduced lifestyle disruption (from both the disease and the treatment). Patients gravitate toward whatever option promises the most control with the least change. For Bimzelx, fast onset of action and high clearance rates serve this need well; for IL-23s, infrequent dosing and established track record serve it differently.
2. Cognitive Overload: Patients struggle with too much data and too many choices. They revert to heuristics (shortcuts) like anchoring on a single metric (injection frequency or efficacy), deferring to the HCP ("what would you do?"), or defaulting to a peer's experience. This is why 23/30 HCPs present only one product -- they intuitively understand that choice overload paralyzes patients.
3. Base Rate Neglect / Social Proof: Patients weight a single anecdotal experience (especially from a known peer) more heavily than large-scale clinical data. One patient rejected Skyrizi because "you talked to one of your friends who had been on Skyrizi for something else, and they had a bad experience." No amount of PASI data could overcome this single peer report. Conversely, HCPs leverage social proof by saying "I've used this a lot with my patients" to build confidence.
4. Catastrophic Thinking: When patients learn about injectables, they immediately picture the worst-case scenario (painful daily injections). When they hear about side effects, they imagine the most severe form (equating "risk of depressive thoughts" with "I'll commit suicide"). This creates an asymmetric information problem: negative information has outsized impact compared to positive information.
Implication for Bimzelx: The catastrophic thinking pattern is particularly challenging for Bimzelx because of the suicidal ideation label warning. Even though HCPs de-risk it effectively ("one patient in the clinical trial, hard to tell if drug-related"), the mere mention triggers catastrophic thinking in patients. UCB needs HCPs to frame this disclosure within a broader safety context that pre-empts catastrophic interpretation.

Pattern 7: Manufacturer Interaction Has Limited Prescribing Conversion
Data from the screener/survey component: HCPs reported manufacturer interactions across multiple companies, with AbbVie and Eli Lilly most frequently cited, followed by Janssen, Amgen, Novartis, BMS, Pfizer, and UCB.
UCB-specific finding: 12 of 30 HCPs reported a UCB (Bimzelx) interaction in the past month. Distribution was even across segments: 4 Bimzelx Believers, 4 Dabblers, 4 Convert IL-17A Writers. Of these 12, only 4 prescribed Bimzelx -- a 33% conversion rate from recent interaction to Rx.
Messages discussed during UCB interactions: Efficacy (PASI scores, quick onset, high ACR), indication coverage (PsO, PsA), safety, coverage/prior authorization, and patient assistance programs.
What this signals: A single rep interaction is insufficient to shift prescribing behavior, particularly when competing against entrenched familiarity bias. The 8 HCPs who had UCB interactions but did not prescribe Bimzelx cited safety concerns, needle aversion, and access challenges -- the same barriers documented in the conversation analysis. This suggests that rep interactions are not effectively addressing the specific barriers that prevent conversion.

Synthesis: The Commercial Opportunity Framework
